episodes.episode_title: #527 Oncology Potpourri for the Hospitalist

pearl: Most patients with brain metastases can still receive therapeutic anticoagulation when clinically indicated, but hospitalists should use extra caution and involve interdisciplinary discussion for malignancies with particularly hemorrhagic brain metastases, including melanoma, renal cell carcinoma, choriocarcinoma, and thyroid cancer.

episodes.episode_title: #527 Oncology Potpourri for the Hospitalist

pearl: Patients with active malignancy and VTE generally require extended-duration anticoagulation as long as the cancer remains active or treatment is ongoing, though some patients may later transition to reduced-dose DOAC therapy during the extended treatment phase depending on bleeding risk and overall clinical context.

episodes.episode_title: #527 Oncology Potpourri for the Hospitalist

pearl: Surgery is rarely beneficial in patients with diffuse carcinomatosis, poor functional status, or recurrent malignant SBO; early palliative care involvement is essential in these scenarios.

episodes.episode_title: #527 Oncology Potpourri for the Hospitalist

pearl: Total parenteral nutrition is usually not beneficial in advanced malignant SBO and should only be considered in carefully selected patients with meaningful expected survival and potential for further cancer-directed therapy.

episodes.episode_title: #527 Oncology Potpourri for the Hospitalist

evidence_links.rationale: Systematic review shows no consistent QOL/survival benefit of TPN in advanced malignant bowel obstruction.

pearl: CAR-T and bispecific T-cell engager (BiTE) therapy therapy can cause cytokine release syndrome, which will present with a sepsis-like illness 6-12 hours after infusion with symptoms including fever, tachycardia, and can progress to shock. The temptation is to treat like sepsis, but appropriate treatment needed is instead tocilizumab or steroids. This is an oncologic emergency – wake up your local hematologist/oncologist for this! (

pearl: Emphasize progress, not perfection. Sustainable change starts with small adjustments that build gradually over time. Remind patients that exercises can be modified to their ability.

pearl: The pelvic floor is critical to bladder, bowel and sexual function. Dysregulation of pelvic floor musculature can lead to a wide array of pathology including chronic pain, incontinence, constipation and erectile dysfunction. Pelvic floor PT can help with all of these processes.

pearl: A flat glucose trace is not the same thing as low cardiovascular risk. If a dietary pattern lowers glucose excursions but raises ApoB, that may represent a dangerous tradeoff.

Resistance training as cornerstone; muscle acts as glucose sink improving insulin sensitivity.

pearl: VO2 max and muscle mass are not vanity metrics. They help clinicians and patients work backwards from the functional goals of older age.

pearl: Clinicians should remain cognizant of these limitations, as the predictive accuracy of specific tools can fluctuate significantly depending on a patient’s background.

episodes.episode_title: 518: Cardiology Meets Longevity

evidence_links.rationale: Blumenthal 2026 supports CAC use for statin decisions when uncertain.

pearl: In patients with high triglycerides, insulin resistance, central adiposity, or metabolic syndrome, LDL-C can underestimate atherogenic particle burden; when LDL-C and ApoB are discordant, risk generally tracks more closely with ApoB.

Non-HDL-C better in obesity/metabolic syndrome, related to particle burden underestimation.

pearl: It should not be overinterpreted as a stand-alone diagnosis, but it can help identify patients whose risk is being underestimated by a routine lab review.

episodes.episode_title: 518: Cardiology Meets Longevity

pearl: Risk calculators are starting points, not final answers.

pearl: The definition of infertility is not just the traditional one of 12 or more months of unprotected heterosexual intercourse without achieving pregnancy in women under age 35 or for 6 or more months in a woman age 35 or older. PCPs should be aware that patients who have underlying medical conditions that impact fertility (PCOS, endometriosis, sexual dysfunction) should not be held to these timelines and should get sooner evaluation. And patients who require donor gametes/embryos also qualify as having infertility sooner.

pearl: Clinical assessment is vital: patients with a convincing history can increase or decrease the pretest probability that a patient is hypogonadal, thus influencing your interpretation of borderline values.

pearl: Decreased libido is the most specific symptom of hypogonadism, especially when it is acute onset. Make sure patients are being realistic and not simply comparing themselves to when they were younger.

episodes.episode_title: #462 Male Hypogonadism Pearls with Dr. Bradley Anawalt: Insights on Testosterone Deficiency

pearl: Free testosterone is rarely discrepant from total testosterone, but may happen in cases where sex-hormone binding globulin (SHBG) is altered such as obesity (lowers the SHBG, thus potentially showing a normal free testosterone even when total appears low) or old age (elevates the SHBG, thus possibly causing a normal total testosterone with low free testosterone).

episodes.episode_title: #462 Male Hypogonadism Pearls with Dr. Bradley Anawalt: Insights on Testosterone Deficiency

evidence_links.rationale: Antonio 2016 shows aging raises SHBG and lowers free testosterone more than total.
Gyawali 2018 shows metabolic syndrome lowers SHBG causing low total with normal free T.
Liang 2021 explains SHBG elevation affecting free vs total testosterone.

pearl: Labs that directly measure free testosterone are erroneous and should not be used. Free testosterone should be calculated (most commonly done) or measured by equilibrium dialysis (expensive, rarely done).

episodes.episode_title: #462 Male Hypogonadism Pearls with Dr. Bradley Anawalt: Insights on Testosterone Deficiency

evidence_links.rationale: Jasuja 2022 validates equilibrium dialysis as accurate free T measurement.
Wang 2009 supports calculated free T and states direct measurements are not useful.

pearl: Many symptoms of hypogonadism are nonspecific, and it is important to consider other etiologies such as depression.

episodes.episode_title: #462 Male Hypogonadism Pearls with Dr. Bradley Anawalt: Insights on Testosterone Deficiency

pearl: Patients with signs and symptoms of low testosterone should be diagnosed with testosterone deficiency only when AM fasting testosterone is low on two occasions, using CDC validated laboratory methods whenever possible.

episodes.episode_title: #462 Male Hypogonadism Pearls with Dr. Bradley Anawalt: Insights on Testosterone Deficiency

pearl: There is evidence in the literature that lifestyle modification (i.e. weight loss via diet and exercise) improves many symptoms of testosterone deficiency in men who have a high BMI, low serum testosterone, and no identifiable disorder of the hypothalamus-pituitary- testicular axis.

episodes.episode_title: #462 Male Hypogonadism Pearls with Dr. Bradley Anawalt: Insights on Testosterone Deficiency

pearl: Using accurate assays are vital to the diagnosis of hypogonadism, especially if presenting symptoms are vague and initial values are borderline.

pearl: Identifying patients with meniscus tears who would benefit from surgery can be complex. Factors that favor surgical intervention are acute tears versus chronic or degenerative tears, younger patients versus older, and lack of concurrent osteoarthritis of the knee.

pearl: Dr. DeStefano covered a wide swath of content around cirrhosis and coagulopathy, describing that hemostasis in cirrhosis is rebalanced, with changes occurring in both procoagulant and anticoagulant factors. It can therefore be hard to tell where on the pro-bleeding to pro-clotting spectrum these patients are. Labs such as PT/INR, PTT, Platelet count, and fibrinogen are not reliable in determining the risk of bleeding or clotting in these patients and should not be targets for repletion in clinically stable patients (“Don’t treat a number” is the general recommendation, here). Administration of blood products on the basis of labs can alter normal homeostasis in these patients and also cause volume shifts, worsening of portal hypertension, and lead to cirrhosis decompensation.

pearl: Available Hepatitis B vaccination formulations include the traditional three vaccine series, a combination HBV / HAV vaccine, the new PreHevbrio vaccine, and the CpG-adjuvanted vaccine. pearl: Antiphospholipid antibody laboratory testing indicates whether antibodies are present that recognize clotting proteins, not that they actually have the special property that enables them to activate the clotting cascade. Thus, false positive testing is a challenge. In particular, transient false positives are common in the setting of acute inflammation, which is why diagnostic criteria require that testing is persistently positive for at least 12 weeks.

episodes.episode_title: #421 Antiphospholipid Syndrome with Dr. Arielle Langer

pearl: Patients may have thrombotic APLS only, obstetric APLS only, or both, depending on whether their antiphospholipid antibodies recognize clotting proteins only, placental trophoblasts only, or both.

pearl: Patients with a history of thrombotic APLS should be switched from warfarin to enoxaparin for the duration of a pregnancy. Patients with a history of obstetric APLS only should be managed during a future pregnancy with aspirin +/- prophylactic-dose enoxaparin (guidelines vary).

pearl: Several trials indicate that warfarin is superior to DOACs for treatment of APLS. For patients who are experiencing breakthrough thrombosis on warfarin, some options include addition of aspirin to warfarin, switching to enoxaparin, addition of hydroxychloroquine, or addition of eculizumab.

pearl: Three clinical buckets that raise suspicion for APLS include patients who are clotting through anticoagulation, patients clotting in unusual locations, or patients with a history of autoimmunity. These three buckets also help inform when to perform antiphospholipid antibody laboratory testing.

pearl: A non-contrast CT scan allows assessment of the density of the adrenal mass, which provides information about whether the mass is lipid-rich (<10 Hounsfield units) or lipid-poor (>10 Hounsfield units.) Lipid-rich masses are most likely benign adenomas and are highly unlikely to be cancer or pheochromocytomas, while lipid-poor masses may be a primary adrenal malignancy, metastatic cancer, pheochromocytoma, or a lipid-poor benign adenoma.

episodes.episode_title: #377 Adrenal Incidentalomas with Dr. William Young

evidence_links.rationale: Young 2007 supports non-contrast CT attenuation distinguishing lipid-rich from lipid-poor masses.
Corwin 2022 discusses limited utility of contrast wash-out, informing which imaging features matter.

pearl: All patients with adrenal incidentalomas should be screened for subclinical Cushing’s syndrome with ACTH, DHEAS, and cortisol levels. If DHEAS is >100 mcg/dL, this suggests that the patient does not have subclinical Cushing’s and the evaluation can stop, while if DHEAS is <100 mcg/dL, the evaluation should proceed with a 1 mg overnight dexamethasone suppression test. Dr. Young also recommends that all patients with a positive 1 mg overnight dexamethasone suppression test undergo a confirmatory 8 mg overnight dexamethasone suppression test prior to surgical intervention, to avoid false positives.

episodes.episode_title: #377 Adrenal Incidentalomas with Dr. William Young

evidence_links.rationale: Carafone 2021 supports DHEAS use in evaluating subclinical Cushing's.
Dennedy 2017 supports DHEAS as biomarker to decide whether further Cushing's testing is needed.
Chiodini 2009 provides rationale for why evaluating subclinical Cushing's matters.
Kebebew 2021 describes subclinical Cushing's clinical features.

pearl: Dr. Young’s expert opinion is that all patients with a lipid-rich mass should receive one follow-up scan at 1 year. Patients with clearly lipid-poor masses (e.g. unenhanced CT attenuation >20 Hounsfield units) should undergo surgical intervention or close monitoring with indefinite follow-up.

episodes.episode_title: #377 Adrenal Incidentalomas with Dr. William Young

evidence_links.rationale: Iniguez-Ariza 2017 supports that imaging phenotype over size guides surgical decisions.

pearl: Patients with adrenal incidentalomas who are hypertensive or hypokalemic should undergo screening for primary hyperaldosteronism with morning aldosterone and renin levels.

pearl: Patients with adrenal incidentalomas with an unenhanced CT attenuation >10 Hounsfield units should undergo screening for pheochromocytoma with plasma fractionated metanephrines.

pearl: The two major questions when evaluating an adrenal incidentaloma are whether the mass is malignant and whether it is functioning (producing hormones.)

pearl: Monkeypox has circulated endemically in West / Central Africa for decades. However, the current outbreak is unique as it has involved cases without links to endemic regions or zoonotic exposures, and case clusters have mapped onto sexual networks.

episodes.episode_title: #359 Monkeypox with Dr. Boghuma Titanji

evidence_links.rationale: Documents historic endemic circulation in West/Central Africa since the 1970s.
Outbreak epidemiology showing 98% cases in MSM, mapping onto sexual networks.

pearl: Supportive care is central to the management of monkeypox, including proper hydration, skin protectants, and NSAIDs and antihistamines. Opiates and IV NSAIDs can be used for severe pain; steroids should be avoided.

pearl: The clinical presentation of monkeypox observed in the current outbreak differs from the “textbook” historic presentation of monkeypox in several ways, including a reduced incubation period, less frequent prodromal symptoms, a higher likelihood of singular or several lesions rather than a generalized rash, and a notable prevalence of isolated proctitis or pharyngitis.

episodes.episode_title: #359 Monkeypox with Dr. Boghuma Titanji

evidence_links.rationale: Details clinical presentation differences in the current outbreak vs textbook.

pearl: There are three available antiviral agents for monkeypox: tecovirimat, brincidofovir, and cidofovir. All of these agents have yet to be studied for monkeypox in randomized controlled trials in humans. Brincidofovir and cidofovir should only be used with expert infectious disease consultation, and are notably teratogenic.

pearl: There are two available vaccines for monkeypox, both based on the vaccinia virus. The first is a live, attenuated, non-replicating form of vaccinia given in two doses 28 days apart. The second is a live, replication-competent form of vaccinia, and is administered via “scarification”; importantly, the live vaccine should not be given to immunocompromised patients.

pearl: First question when dealing with hypoxemia: Sick or not sick? That will guide your approach. Next, use history, exam, labs/rads and POCUS to help further evaluate your patient.

pearl: Hypoxemia refers to low oxygen content in the blood, however, what’s more clinically relevant is hypoxia which suggests reduced oxygen delivery and consequently, inadequate oxygen at the tissue-level, which can result in end-organ dysfunction/damage.

episodes.episode_title: #350 Rapid Response Series:Acute Hypoxemia

evidence_links.rationale: Hoiland oxygen content equation explains oxygen delivery to tissues, the basis for distinguishing hypoxia from hypoxemia.

pearl: Pulse oximeters are imperfect tools – interpret the results with caution! Similarly, blood gasses are helpful and have their place… in the right clinical scenario. Not every patient needs an ABG or VBG if they are hypoxemic!

episodes.episode_title: #350 Rapid Response Series:Acute Hypoxemia

evidence_links.rationale: Nitzan explains pulse oximeter function and limitations, directly supporting cautious interpretation.
Sjoding shows pulse oximeters miss occult hypoxemia in Black patients, supporting the caution claim.

pearl: Remember the SIX major causes for hypoxemia. Shunt, V/Q mismatch and diffusion limitation feature reductions in the A-a difference. All causes of hypoxemia will correct – at least to some degree – with supplemental oxygen, EXCEPT SHUNTING!

episodes.episode_title: #350 Rapid Response Series:Acute Hypoxemia

pearl: Supplemental oxygen is a powerful treatment modality with many delivery systems – be familiar with the options and indications!

episodes.episode_title: #350 Rapid Response Series:Acute Hypoxemia

evidence_links.rationale: FLORALI supports HFNC as a delivery system option in acute hypoxemic respiratory failure.

pearl: … BUT, treat the patient first! There is no reason NOT to put the patient on supplemental oxygen while performing your evaluation. In fact, it can be therapeutic AND diagnostic!

pearl: Endocrine complications of checkpoint inhibitors include hypothyroidism (more common than hyperthyroidism), adrenal insufficiency, and new onset insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus.

episodes.episode_title: #342 Checkpoint Inhibitors:

pearl: Ensure your patients have baseline labs (TSH, FT4, AM cortisol, basic metabolic panel, glucose, liver function tests, and a complete blood count) before starting checkpoint inhibitors.

episodes.episode_title: #342 Checkpoint Inhibitors:

pearl: Immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICIs) turn off the pause button on the immune response, and keep T-cells (and the immune response against cancer cells) active.

episodes.episode_title: #342 Checkpoint Inhibitors:

pearl: Immunotherapy harnesses the body’s own immune system to target cancer cells. The side effect profile is different from traditional chemotherapy, founded in the inflammation that ensues when the immune system is turned on.

episodes.episode_title: #342 Checkpoint Inhibitors:

pearl: Immunotherapy has a century-plus long history, and is based on the principle that the immune system can attack specific “bad” or “other” cells, including tumor cells.

episodes.episode_title: #342 Checkpoint Inhibitors:

pearl: Rheumatologic complications include seronegative sicca syndrome, inflammatory arthritis (typically negative for RF, CCP, and HLA-B27), and myositis.

episodes.episode_title: #342 Checkpoint Inhibitors:

evidence_links.rationale: Allenback 2020 covers ICI-associated myositis with proximal weakness and elevated CK.

Ramos-Casals 2019 details sicca syndrome related to but distinct from Sjogren's.

pearl: The adverse effects from checkpoint inhibitors are byproducts of inflammation, and have parallels in autoimmune disorders of a variety of organs. They can occur at any point after initiation with checkpoint inhibitors, even years after stopping.

episodes.episode_title: #342 Checkpoint Inhibitors:

evidence_links.rationale: Haanen 2020 addresses recurrence of irAEs on restarting therapy.

pearl: There are two overall types of checkpoint inhibitors used across a variety of cancer types, ones that target CTLA-4, and ones that target PD-1/PD-L1. The most common of them are pembrolizumab and nivolumab (which target PD-1), and ipilimumab (which targets CTLA-4).

episodes.episode_title: #342 Checkpoint Inhibitors:

pearl: Typical presentation of biliary pain: moderate-severe pain, which may be diffuse abdominal/epigastric or localized to the right upper quadrant and may radiate to the back. Typically pain episodes last from 30 minutes to several hours. Biliary pain classically should not include dyspepsia, bloating, or gas but is frequently associated with nausea.

pearl: Up to 40% of patients may have continued pain after a cholecystectomy, so it is important to confirm that the abdominal pain your patient is experiencing is truly from gallstones before surgery (and not blame their functional dyspepsia on incidentally noted gallstones).

pearl: A creatinine “bump” with diuresis does not necessarily signify kidney injury. Allow for “permissive hypercreatinemia” while diuresing. Avoid stopping diuresis prematurely with residual congestion on board simply because of a rise in creatinine.

ESCAPE: hemoconcentration paradoxically associated with reduced morbidity/mortality.
ROSE-AHF: creatinine changes not correlated with kidney injury biomarkers during decongestion.

pearl: Cardiac biomarkers such as troponin and BNP are often chronically elevated in patients with CKD, and correlate with worse prognosis. A relative change in BNP from baseline can be informative in patients in CKD.

Michos 2014: CKD with elevated biomarkers worse outcomes.

pearl: For an initial blood pressure regimen, low-dose combination therapy is preferable to single agent therapy. A low-dose calcium channel blocker + ARB is a good starting regimen. Choose ARBs over ACE inhibitors due to their superior side effect profile. ARBs should be first-line in blood pressure management..

episodes.episode_title: #321 Hypertension FAQ: Common Outpatient Cases with Dr. Jordy Cohen

evidence_links.rationale: Chen 2021 supports preferring ARBs over ACEis for superior side-effect profile.

Elliot 1996 evidence of higher cough/angioedema with ACEis favoring ARBs.
Miller 2008 evidence of higher ACEi adverse effects supporting ARB choice.
ALLHAT post-hoc analyses relevant to CCB/ARB starting choice discussion.

pearl: For patients with chronic kidney disease, fear of a creatinine bump should not discourage providers from using ACEis/ARBs; on the contrary, these medications are protective in patients with kidney disease. Up to a 30% rise in creatinine with initiation of an ACEi/ARB is acceptable. Close monitoring of potassium is important, with a potassium threshold of 5.5.

episodes.episode_title: #321 Hypertension FAQ: Common Outpatient Cases with Dr. Jordy Cohen

evidence_links.rationale: KDIGO guidelines state up to 30% creatinine rise acceptable with ACEi/ARB.

Mann 2001 shows cardiovascular benefit of RAAS blockade in CKD.

pearl: Given the difficulties with obtaining high-quality standardized blood pressure readings in the office, out-of-office home blood pressure measurement is recommended to confirm a diagnosis of hypertension. Patients should be instructed to acquire a validated blood pressure machine and educated on the steps of taking their blood pressure. A minimum of three days of home blood pressure readings (two back-to-back readings every AM and two every PM) is sufficient.

pearl: In a patient with refractory hypertension, primary hyperaldosteronism should be on the differential. Testing serum renin and aldosterone does not require stopping your patient’s anti-hypertensive medications (with the exception of mineralocorticoid antagonists (MRA) and amiloride).

Arvanitakis review reinforces limited efficacy of dementia pharmacotherapy.
Parsons Cochrane review of stopping meds showed decline not clinically significant, reinforcing marginal benefit.

pearl: In rare cases, antipsychotics are needed to manage dangerous dementia-related behaviors (though this is not the first-line treatment!) If needed, use at the lowest dose possible for the shortest duration. The number needed to harm (where harm is an associated death) is 26-50.

pearl: SGLT-2 inhibitors lower blood sugar without significant hypoglycemia, encourage weight loss, and lower blood pressure. They reduce heart failure complications, slow progression of CKD, reduce CVD, and lower death rates.

pearl: High altitude sickness is a spectrum of illness that includes acute mountain sickness and that can progress to high altitude cerebral edema; High altitude pulmonary edema is a separate altitude-related clinical entity. AMS and HACE can be prevented by slow ascent or prophylaxis with acetazolamide 125mg BID. Treatment includes acetazolamide 250mg BID or dexamethasone.

episodes.episode_title: #242 Wilderness Medicine: Prepare for Disaster

pearl: Long feared for the risk of limb loss, tourniquets now have a role in triage in the field and are an essential part of emergency kits. Advances in local hemostasis such as bandages impregnated with procoagulants are additionally available.

pearl: Many injuries and adverse events in the wilderness start with assuming “best case scenario” or taking shortcuts. Always prepare for the unexpected, including the need to spend the night in the wilderness.

pearl: Hypoosmolar-hyponatremia is most common, which means that the low sodium and osmolarity are concordant- this can be ADH dependent (CHF, SIADH, etc) or independent (tea-and-toast, primary polydipsia, oliguric kidney failure) .

episodes.episode_title: REBOOT #48 Hyponatremia Deconstructed

pearl: Low sodium with high osmolality is due to high levels of an additional osmolite, most likely glucose, drawing water into the blood.

episodes.episode_title: REBOOT #48 Hyponatremia Deconstructed

evidence_links.rationale: Hillier and Barrett for correcting sodium in hyperglycemia.

pearl: Low sodium with normal osmolality is likely a lab error- high protein can interfere with sodium measurement. The cause of high protein should be investigated (ex: IVIG, lipid panel, or serum protein electrophoresis). This is pseudohyponatremia.

episodes.episode_title: REBOOT #48 Hyponatremia Deconstructed

pearl: Normal saline can improve hyponatremia due to hypovolemia, but it worsens hypervolemic-hyponatremia (ex: heart failure) and can lower the sodium in SIADH.

episodes.episode_title: REBOOT #48 Hyponatremia Deconstructed

pearl: SSRIs, anti-epileptics, sulfonylureas, and opioids can lead to SIADH mediated hyponatremia.

episodes.episode_title: REBOOT #48 Hyponatremia Deconstructed

evidence_links.rationale: Shepshelovich directly supports SSRIs, antiseizure, sulfonylureas, opioids causing SIADH.
Liamis cited as evidence medications can cause SIADH-mediated hyponatremia.

pearl: Serum sodium is used as a marker for serum tonicity. While serum sodium and osmolality generally agree, this is not always the case.

episodes.episode_title: REBOOT #48 Hyponatremia Deconstructed

evidence_links.rationale: Adrogué and Madias referenced for sodium and tonicity not always agreeing.
Rondon-Berrios cited to illustrate low sodium with high tonicity coexisting.

pearl: Tea-and-toast or beer potomania hyponatremia occur when low solute intake limits the ability of the kidneys to produce urine that maintains electrolyte homeostasis.
